People should not be blamed for their parentage, as long as they use their own abilities to overcome any shame. God had forgiven Israel, therefore Jephthah will forgive. He does not speak with confidence about his success, knowing how justly God might allow the Ammonites to prevail to further punish Israel. Nor does he speak with any confidence at all in himself. If he succeeds, it is because the Lord delivers them into his hand; he reminds his countrymen to look to God as the one who gives victory. The same question is put to those who desire salvation through Christ. If he saves you, will you be willing that he should rule you? He will save you on no other terms. If he makes you happy, will he make you holy? If he is your helper, will he be your head? Jephthah was willing to risk his life for a little worldly honor; should we be discouraged in our Christian battle by the difficulties we may face, when Christ has promised a crown of life to him who overcomes?

One example of the honor and respect we owe to God, as our God, is rightly using what he gives us. Receive it from him, use it for him, and give it up when he calls for it. The whole of this message shows that Jephthah was well acquainted with the books of Moses. His argument was clear, and his demand reasonable. Those who have the most courageous faith will be the most willing to seek peace and ready to make advances to obtain; but greed and ambition often hide their plans under a claim of fairness, and render peaceful efforts useless.

Several important lessons can be learned from Jephthah's vow. 1. There may be remainders of distrust and doubt, even in the hearts of true and great believers. 2. Our vows to God should not be as a way to purchase the favor we desire, but to express gratitude to him. 3. We need to think carefully before making vows, lest we entangle ourselves. 4. What we have solemnly vowed to God, we must perform, if it is possible and lawful, even though it is difficult and painful for us. 5. Children should obediently and cheerfully submit to their parents in the Lord. It is hard to say what Jephthah did to fulfill his vow; but it is thought that he did not offer his daughter as a burnt-offering. Such a sacrifice would have been an abomination to the Lord; it is supposed she was obliged to remain unmarried and apart from her family. Concerning this and some other passages in sacred history, about which learned men are divided and in doubt, we need not trouble ourselves; what is necessary for our salvation is plain enough, thanks be to God. If the reader remembers Christ’s promise concerning the teaching of the Holy Spirit, and places himself under this heavenly Teacher, the Holy Ghost will guide to all truth in every passage, so far as it needs to be understood.
